text.title: headless-cli-four-guards Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for headless agent-CLI invocations.

rule.statement: Every non-interactive invocation of claude/codex/aider/opencode MUST set ALL FOUR guards: (a) print/headless flag, (b) explicit `--allowedTools`, (c) `--max-turns`, (d) `< /dev/null` stdin redirect.
rule.rationale: Each missing guard maps to a documented failure mode (TUI hang, permission stall, runaway loop, stdin garbage). The four-guard rule is the empirical minimum.

rule.statement: Production invocations MUST NOT use `--dangerously-skip-permissions`; the allowlist must be explicit even if it requires enumerating tools.
rule.rationale: Skipping permissions removes guard #2. The agent can run arbitrary destructive tools — bash, git push, network calls — with no upper bound.

rule.statement: `--max-turns` MUST be a value tied to a real workflow length (typically 15-30 for a coding task); values above 100 are forbidden in production scripts.
rule.rationale: Open caps drain budget within minutes when the agent enters a tool-call loop. A meaningful cap also acts as an alarm.

rule.statement: Each CLI has its own flag mapping that MUST be applied: Claude Code (`-p`, `--allowedTools`, `--max-turns`, `< /dev/null`); Codex (`codex exec`, `--sandbox`, `timeout` wrap, `< /dev/null`); Aider (`--yes`, `--auto-test off`, `--max-chat-history-tokens`, `< /dev/null`); opencode (headless flag, tool config, run-time cap, `< /dev/null`).
rule.rationale: Each CLI uses different flag names; mapping must be explicit so the lint can verify each invocation.

rule.statement: CI MUST run `scripts/validate-headless-cli-four-guards.py` on every shell script that invokes an agent CLI; absence of any guard MUST fail the build.
rule.rationale: Code review alone misses the missing-guard case. Lint enforces the rule mechanically.
